docs.title: Welcome to RUNE
docs.text: RUNE (Remediation & Unified Node Executor) is a modular orchestration tool built for Linux environments. It lets security and platform teams quickly respond to system alerts by triggering automated actions—like gathering logs or restarting services—across remote systems via CLI or dashboard.

docs.title: What is RUNE?
docs.text:
RUNE is designed to:

  • Execute predefined remote actions safely and consistently
  • Return structured, machine-readable results
  • Be agentless by default, using SSH or SSM
  • Support plugins written in Bash or Python

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Actions: Discrete operations like restart-docker or gather-logs
  • Plugins: Scripts that implement those actions, conforming to a communication spec
  • LOM/LMM: Python core modules that coordinate execution and capture results

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Explore these key documents:

  • Plugin Development Guide
  • System Architecture
  • Runtime Communication Spec (RCS)
  • API Reference

Want to help? Contribute or follow the Roadmap to see what's next.

docs.title: Why RUNE?
docs.text:
Because clicking a button should be all it takes to recover a system.

Simple to run. Safe to trust. Built to grow.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE API Reference (Planned Interface)
docs.text: This document outlines the future RUNE REST API for triggering actions, introspecting available plugins, and querying status. It is not required for MVP, but defines the planned structure for dashboard and programmatic integration.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text:
POST /v1/run
GET  /v1/actions
GET  /v1/status/:job_id

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• Bearer token or mutual TLS (future)
  • Headers:

http Authorization: Bearer <token> Content-Type: application/json

docs.title: POST /v1/run
docs.text: Trigger a remote action on a node.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "action": "gather-logs",
  "node": "host-123",
  "parameters": {
    "duration": "5m"
  },
  "trace_id": "optional-guid-here"
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "job_id": "abc123",
  "status": "submitted",
  "trace_id": "abc123",
  "message": "Action accepted"
}

docs.title: GET /v1/actions
docs.text: List available actions (via MRS metadata).

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
  {
    "name": "restart-docker",
    "description": "Restarts Docker service",
    "input_schema": {...},
    "output_schema": {...}
  }
]

docs.title: GET /v1/status/:job_id
docs.text: Retrieve execution status and results.

docs.title: Response (Success)
docs.text:
{
  "job_id": "abc123",
  "status": "complete",
  "result": {
    "output_data": {
      "archive": "/tmp/logs.tar.gz"
    },
    "exit_code": 0
  }
}

docs.title: Response (Failure)
docs.text:
{
  "job_id": "abc123",
  "status": "failed",
  "error": {
    "code": -32001,
    "message": "Non-zero exit code",
    "data": {
      "plugin": "restart-nomad",
      "exit_code": 1
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Execution is asynchronous
  • job_id and trace_id are UUIDs
  • Internally routes through LOM → LMM → plugin → back to client
  • Response conforms to RCS or EPS

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Glossary of Terms
docs.text: A reference for acronyms, modules, and naming conventions used throughout the RUNE framework.

docs.title: RUNE
docs.text: Remediation & Unified Node Executor — The name of the framework. Modular, CLI-driven orchestration engine for Linux environments.

docs.title: LOM
docs.text: Lifecycle Orchestration Module — Handles incoming execution requests, validates inputs, selects the correct plugin, and delegates to the LMM.

docs.title: LMM
docs.text: Local Mediation Module — Executes plugins remotely over SSH or SSM, captures output, enforces structured communication, and returns results.

docs.title: RCS
docs.text: Runtime Communication Specification — The protocol that defines how action execution messages are structured and routed.

docs.title: EPS
docs.text: Error Protocol Specification — Defines how errors are captured, formatted, and routed inside the RUNE system.

docs.title: MRS
docs.text: Module Registration Specification — Used to declare plugin/module capabilities (name, schema, version) for introspection and validation.

docs.title: Plugin
docs.text: A standalone Bash or Python script that conforms to the RUNE protocol and performs a specific system-level operation.

docs.title: BPCS
docs.text: Bash Plugin Communication Specification — Contract that all Bash-based plugins must follow. Input via stdin, output via stdout in JSON.

docs.title: CLI
docs.text: Command Line Interface — The rune terminal command used to run actions like rune run restart-docker --node web01

docs.title: SSM
docs.text: AWS Systems Manager — Optional agent-based transport method for executing remote commands without SSH.

docs.title: Trace ID / Span ID
docs.text: Unique identifiers used to track actions across distributed components and log trails.

docs.title: Job ID
docs.text: A unique identifier representing one execution instance of a plugin.

docs.title: Action
docs.text: The logical name of an operation that RUNE can execute, such as gather-logs, restart-docker, or noop.

docs.title: Plugin Directory
docs.text: Location where registered plugins reside. Example: plugins/ folder under project root.

docs.title: Payload
docs.text: The body of a structured JSON message that includes the data to be passed into or out of an action execution.

docs.title: exit code
docs.text:
Integer returned by a plugin indicating success (0) or failure (1+). Must be consistent with structured output.

This glossary will evolve as RUNE expands. PRs welcome for new terms.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Plugin Development Guide for RUNE
docs.text: This guide explains how to create, test, and register Bash- or Python-based plugins for RUNE's MVP framework.

docs.title: Plugin Overview
docs.text:
Plugins are standalone scripts executed on remote systems via SSH or SSM. Each plugin performs a discrete action (e.g., gather logs, restart a service) and must conform to RUNE’s Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS) by reading structured JSON from stdin and returning structured JSON to stdout.

Supported languages:

  • ✅ Bash (primary for MVP)
  • ⬜️ Python (planned)

docs.title: Plugin Structure (Bash Example)
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Read stdin into a variable
INPUT=$(cat)

# Extract data (example using jq)
PARAMS=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.payload.data.input_parameters')
HOSTNAME=$(hostname)

# Main logic (example: archive logs)
LOG_FILE="/tmp/syslog_$HOSTNAME.tar.gz"
tar -czf "$LOG_FILE" /var/log/syslog || {
  echo "{\"error\": \"Log archive failed\"}" && exit 1
}

# Return structured output
cat <<EOF
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "archive": "$LOG_FILE"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}
EOF

docs.title: Required Behavior
docs.text:
• Read structured JSON from stdin
  • Return JSON to stdout with keys: message_metadata, payload, and error
  • Exit 0 on success, non-zero on error
  • Avoid writing logs to stdout—use stderr

docs.title: Plugin Naming
docs.text:
• Use lowercase kebab-case: restart-docker.sh, noop.sh
  • Filename is treated as the plugin identifier
  • File must be executable (chmod +x plugin.sh)

docs.title: Testing Plugins
docs.text:
To test a plugin:

echo '{
  "payload": {
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": {}
    }
  }
}' | ./restart-docker.sh | jq .

Check:

  • Valid JSON output
  • Correct exit code
  • No junk on stdout

docs.title: Registration
docs.text:
LOM/LMM may eventually auto-register plugins (see MRS). For MVP:

  • Plugins are statically mapped in RUNE core
  • Place plugins in plugins/ directory

Future version will support dynamic registration with metadata.

docs.title: Output Example (Success)
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": {
    "result": "success",
    "output_data": {
      "archive": "/tmp/syslog_web01.tar.gz"
    }
  },
  "error": null
}

docs.title: Output Example (Failure)
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {},
  "payload": null,
  "error": {
    "message": "Log archive failed",
    "code": 1001
  }
}

For plugin execution and message format, see /docs/protocol_runtime.md (RCS) and /docs/protocol_error.md (EPS).

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: EPS defines how errors are reported, formatted, routed, and remediated within the RUNE framework. This protocol ensures that all failure modes—whether from remote plugins, internal modules, or transport failures—return a predictable, machine-parseable structure.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: EPS messages are generated by any RUNE component encountering an error (plugin, LMM, transport, etc.) and routed through the Mediator for centralized handling and logging.

docs.title: Error Message Structure
docs.text:
{
  "routing": {
    "event_type": "EPS_ERROR_RESPONSE",
    "source_module": "mediator",
    "target_module": "error_handler"
  },
  "error_payload": {
    "error_data": {
      "code": -32001,
      "message": "Plugin execution failed",
      "data": {
        "plugin": "restart-docker",
        "host": "web-01",
        "reason": "Non-zero exit code",
        "exit_code": 1
      }
    },
    "error_context": {
      "severity": "medium",
      "recoverable": true,
      "recovery_actions": ["retry", "alert-operator"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Field Descriptions
docs.text:
• event_type: Always set to EPS_ERROR_RESPONSE
  • source_module: Origin of the error (e.g., mediator, transport, plugin)
  • target_module: Optional; default is centralized error handler
  • error_data.code: Integer code (negative)
  • error_data.message: Human-readable summary
  • error_data.data: Arbitrary structured context
  • error_context.severity: low, medium, high, or critical
  • error_context.recoverable: Boolean
  • error_context.recovery_actions: Suggested actions (retry, fallback, alert, etc.)

docs.title: Error Generation Rules
docs.text:
• Any plugin that exits non-zero must emit a structured EPS error (if it can)
  • The mediator will wrap stdout/stderr failures in EPS automatically
  • All transport or execution errors must be normalized to EPS

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• EPS is for machine handling, not end-user output (LMM/LOM can format it)
  • Log output from errors should be associated via message_id or trace_id
  • EPS is routed like RCS messages, but always interpreted as an exception path

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Defines the registration format and process for RUNE modules and plugins. Modules declare their capabilities, supported actions, metadata, and schema versions through this protocol. This specification ensures that the RUNE Orchestrator (LOM) can discover and validate the available actions at runtime.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This protocol is invoked during startup, registration time, or when explicitly requested by the LOM. All modules and plugins must comply with this schema to be discoverable.

docs.title: Registration Payload
docs.text:
{
  "registration_metadata": {
    "module_name": "restart-nomad",
    "module_version": "1.0.0",
    "registered_at": "2025-12-01T00:00:00Z"
  },
  "capabilities": [
    {
      "action": "restart-nomad",
      "description": "Restarts Nomad systemd unit",
      "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {},
        "required": []
      },
      "output_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "result": { "type": "string" }
        },
        "required": ["result"]
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Key Fields
docs.text:
• module_name: Unique, lowercase identifier of the plugin or module
  • module_version: Semantic version of the module or plugin
  • registered_at: RFC 3339 timestamp (UTC)

  • capabilities[]: List of actions this module supports

  • input_schema: JSON Schema object describing expected inputs

  • output_schema: JSON Schema object describing the structure of valid output

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Each module must register exactly once per runtime session
  • Duplicate names will be rejected
  • Version resolution will favor latest for conflicting module names

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• MRS is invoked by LOM directly, not over the runtime channel
  • Capability schemas may be stored for CLI introspection or dashboard use

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Runtime Communication Specification (RCS) defines the JSON message structure used for all operational messaging within RUNE during plugin and module execution. This protocol enables consistent, observable, and traceable communication between the RUNE core (LMM/LOM) and remote plugin runners.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Plugin execution requests and responses
  • Status reporting from remote scripts
  • Log or data result transmission

docs.title: Message Structure
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {
    "version": "1.0",
    "message_id": "<uuid>",
    "created_at": "<rfc3339 UTC timestamp>",
    "correlation_id": "<uuid, optional>"
  },
  "routing": {
    "event_type": "<action_name>",
    "source_module": "<module or CLI identifier>",
    "reply_to": {
      "event_type": "<expected_response_event>",
      "timeout_ms": 3000
    }
  },
  "payload": {
    "schema_version": "rcs_v1",
    "content_type": "application/json",
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": { ... }
    }
  },
  "observability": {
    "trace_id": "<optional-trace-id>"
  }
}

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• All timestamps must be in RFC 3339 UTC format
  • All message_id and correlation_id values must be UUID v4
  • Payload must contain a valid input_parameters object for plugins

docs.title: Minimal Valid Message
docs.text:
{
  "message_metadata": {
    "version": "1.0",
    "message_id": "...",
    "created_at": "..."
  },
  "routing": {
    "event_type": "gather-logs",
    "source_module": "cli"
  },
  "payload": {
    "schema_version": "rcs_v1",
    "content_type": "application/json",
    "data": {
      "input_parameters": {}
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• RCS does not define how plugins are discovered or registered—that is handled via MRS.
  • Error conditions must be reported using EPS format, not RCS.
  • RCS should remain lightweight to support SSH/SSM transmission.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Development Roadmap
docs.text: This document outlines the staged development plan for RUNE, with the goal of balancing rapid MVP delivery against long-term architectural flexibility. Each phase builds on the last, expanding RUNE's capabilities without introducing bloat or unnecessary complexity.

docs.title: 🚀 Phase 1: MVP Delivery (Current Focus)
docs.text: Core goal: Execute healing and diagnostic operations on Linux nodes via CLI and/or dashboard API trigger, using remote plugin invocation over SSH or SSM.

docs.title: 🔧 Deliverables
docs.text:
• [x] rune_cli.py: Command-line interface with run subcommand
  • [x] orchestrator.py: Action validation, plugin resolution
  • [x] mediator.py: Remote plugin runner, I/O router
  • [x] transport_ssh.py: Execute plugin via SSH
  • [ ] transport_ssm.py: Execute plugin via AWS SSM (optional fallback)
  • [x] Bash plugins:
  • gather-logs.sh
  • restart-docker.sh
  • restart-nomad.sh
  • noop.sh
  • [x] Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS v0.1)
  • [x] Structured stdout/stderr/output with JSON validation
  • [x] CLI flags: --dry-run, --output json|pretty, --use-ssm
  • [x] Logging & output capture to local logs or stdout

docs.title: 🧱 Phase 2: Framework Hardening
docs.text: Add developer-quality tooling, formal interfaces, and observability layers to stabilize RUNE as a platform.

docs.title: 🧪 Testing & Dev Tooling
docs.text:
• [ ] Unit test coverage for LOM and LMM
  • [ ] Integration test harness (mock transport layer)
  • [ ] Plugin test framework (stdin → plugin → JSON validator)
  • [ ] Basic telemetry for plugin runtime, failure rate, duration

docs.title: ⚙️ Plugin Runtime Improvements
docs.text:
• [ ] Versioned plugin discovery
  • [ ] Registry of known plugins with metadata (name, description, version, tags)
  • [ ] Environment validation prior to plugin exec (e.g., systemd available?)

docs.title: 🔐 Security / Access Control
docs.text:
• [ ] Execution audit trail (host, user, action, timestamp)
  • [ ] Optional sudo enforcement or policy filtering
  • [ ] CLI session token or execution fingerprint

docs.title: 🌐 Phase 3: Remote API + GUI Integration
docs.text: Enable RUNE to be triggered from external dashboards, alerting systems, or automation platforms.

docs.title: 📡 API & Dashboard Integration
docs.text:
• [ ] REST API to trigger actions (POST /run/<action>)
  • [ ] AuthN/AuthZ layer (token-based or OIDC)
  • [ ] Integration with SoC GUI to trigger RUNE via button
  • [ ] Response output routing to dashboard (WebSocket or polling)

docs.title: 🧠 Phase 4: Workflow & Orchestration Expansion
docs.text: Allow RUNE to execute multi-step workflows, aggregate outputs, and implement conditional or sequential logic.

docs.title: 🔄 Workflow Engine (Lightweight)
docs.text:
• [ ] Sequential action chaining
  • [ ] Conditional branching (if restart failed, gather-logs)
  • [ ] Parallel execution (across nodes)
  • [ ] Reusable workflow definitions in YAML/JSON

docs.title: 🔮 Phase 5: Enterprise Features & Ecosystem
docs.text: Solidify RUNE as a powerful, extensible automation fabric for large-scale Linux infrastructure.

docs.title: 🔐 Security & Policy
docs.text:
• [ ] Role-based policy to restrict plugins/actions per operator
  • [ ] Secrets integration (Vault, AWS SSM Parameter Store)

docs.title: 🌍 Ecosystem & Distribution
docs.text:
• [ ] Plugin packaging and versioning
  • [ ] CLI introspection: rune list-actions, rune plugin-info
  • [ ] Community plugin registry or sharing system

docs.title: 📊 Observability & Reporting
docs.text:
• [ ] Send trace data to ELK/Splunk
  • [ ] Correlation IDs and distributed trace support
  • [ ] Execution metrics dashboard (Grafana/Prometheus-friendly)

docs.title: 🧭 Roadmap Philosophy
docs.text:
• Build fast. Stay lean.
  • Prioritize simplicity, modularity, and explicit behavior.
  • Agentless first, agents only if necessary.
  • Always return structured output—RUNE should be machine- and human-readable.

This roadmap is a living document. Pull requests welcome for additions, edits, or discussion.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Security Model
docs.text: This document outlines the security principles and safeguards embedded in RUNE's MVP design. The goal is to ensure remote actions are controlled, auditable, and constrained by least privilege.

docs.title: Threat Model
docs.text:
RUNE is designed to execute remote commands across Linux systems triggered by operators or automated systems. Key risks:

  • Unauthorized action execution
  • Plugin misuse or privilege escalation
  • Leakage of secrets or sensitive output
  • Insecure transport of execution payloads

docs.title: Security Principles
docs.text:
• Agentless by default: No long-running daemons on endpoints
  • Transport abstraction: SSH/SSM handles identity, encryption, and auth
  • Explicit plugin invocation: No dynamic code injection or reflection
  • Structured I/O: Prevents script injection or sloppy command output parsing

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
• SSH: Leverages host/user SSH keys, sudo restrictions, or bastion models
  • SSM: IAM-based authentication tied to instance profile or SSO identity

docs.title: Authorization (MVP Scope)
docs.text:
• CLI can restrict which users can run which plugins
  • Plugin whitelist enforced by LOM
  • Future: RBAC policy engine tied to user/session metadata

docs.title: Plugin Execution Boundaries
docs.text:
• Plugins must run as non-root unless explicitly whitelisted
  • RUNE can enforce sudo -u restricted_user when calling plugins
  • Output parsing is validated before being accepted

docs.title: Secrets Handling
docs.text:
• No secrets should be embedded in plugin code
  • Plugins should reference external secret stores (e.g. Vault, SSM Parameter Store)
  • Future RUNE version may inject secrets securely via environment or temp file

docs.title: Audit & Logging
docs.text:
• All invocations should log:

  • Timestamp

  • Target node
  • Action invoked

  • Exit status / trace ID

  • Logs written locally and/or sent to centralized observability sink

docs.title: Hardening Recommendations
docs.text:
• Restrict plugin directory access to root or ops group
  • Use SSH key constraints (from=, command=)
  • Use session-level trace ID for every action
  • Disable direct shell access to plugin execution host if possible

RUNE is not a privilege escalator—it assumes least privilege at every layer. All actions must be deliberate, accountable, and traceable.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE System Architecture
docs.text: This document provides a high-level view of RUNE's core components and their interaction during plugin execution. This MVP design favors clarity, modularity, and minimal surface area for deployment.

docs.title: High-Level Architecture (MVP)
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  Dashboard["SoC Dashboard or CLI"] --> CLI["RUNE CLI"]
  CLI --> LOM["LOM (Orchestrator)"]
  LOM --> LMM["LMM (Mediator)"]
  LMM --> SSHorSSM["SSH/SSM Remote Execution"]
  SSHorSSM --> Plugin["Bash Plugin"]
  Plugin --> LMM
  LMM --> LOM
  LOM --> CLI

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
• Primary entrypoint for users and systems
  • Parses commands, invokes LOM

docs.title: LOM – Lifecycle Orchestrator Module
docs.text:
• Validates requested action
  • Selects appropriate plugin
  • Delegates execution to LMM

docs.title: LMM – Local Mediation Module
docs.text:
• Handles all plugin execution and communication
  • Ensures JSON payload validity and formatting
  • Routes structured success/failure messages back to LOM

docs.title: Remote Execution
docs.text:
• Agentless invocation via:

  • SSH with bash -s

  • AWS SSM send-command (optional)

docs.title: Plugins (Remote Scripts)
docs.text:
• Perform the requested task
  • Conform to Bash Plugin Communication Spec (BPCS)
  • Must produce structured JSON and exit codes

docs.title: Message Protocols
docs.text: Purpose Spec Action Request RCS (Runtime Comm) Registration MRS (Module Registry) Error Reporting EPS (Error Protocol)

docs.title: Observability Hooks
docs.text:
• All actions return structured JSON
  • stdout: primary channel for result object
  • stderr: reserved for debug, logging, or trace IDs
  • Optional: logs/telemetry shipped to centralized sink

docs.title: Design Notes
docs.text:
• All modules are decoupled and replaceable
  • No direct plugin-to-plugin interaction
  • Core system is Python-based; plugins can be any language that conforms to the protocol
  • Architecture supports eventual transition to event-driven workflows without overhaul

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: RUNE Testing Guide
docs.text: This guide describes how to validate RUNE core logic, plugin behavior, and overall integration using structured tests.

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
Located in tests/

  • Test rune_cli.py, orchestrator.py, mediator.py
  • Use mocks for SSH/SSM transport to avoid real remote execution

docs.title: Plugin Tests
docs.text:
• Pipe mock input JSON into each plugin
  • Capture and validate structured JSON output
  • Ensure correct exit codes and error handling

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
• Simulate end-to-end execution:

bash rune run restart-docker --node testhost --dry-run

  • Verify orchestration, payload formatting, transport call, and output parsing

docs.title: 🧪 Plugin Testing Example
docs.text:
echo '{
  "payload": { "data": { "input_parameters": {} } }
}' | ./plugins/restart-docker.sh | jq .
  • Output must be valid JSON
  • Exit code 0 for success
  • Exit 1+ for failure with proper error key

docs.title: ✅ Test Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] All core modules have unit test coverage ≥ 80%
  • [ ] Every plugin has a test case and error case
  • [ ] Transport fallbacks (mocked)
  • [ ] CLI flags are respected in tests

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• pytest
  • unittest.mock
  • jq (for CLI-level JSON parsing)
  • GitHub Actions (CI-ready)

Stay strict. RUNE actions must always behave predictably, or not at all.

© 2025 Richard Majewski. Licensed under the MPL-2.0.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project follows PEP-8 with modern Python best practices and some intentional deviations documented below.

docs.title: Line Length
docs.text:
Maximum: 99 characters

Rationale:

  • Modern standard (black/ruff default)
  • Better for modern displays and IDEs
  • Balances readability with practicality
  • Reduces unnecessary line breaks

Note: This is an intentional deviation from strict PEP-8 (79 characters) but aligns with modern Python community practices and tooling defaults.

docs.title: Type Hints
docs.text: Style: Python 3.10+ syntax with from __future__ import annotations

docs.title: Modern Type Syntax
docs.text:
from __future__ import annotations

# Use built-in generics (not typing.List, typing.Dict, etc.)
def process_items(items: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:
    """Process a list of items and return counts."""
    ...

# Use union operator for optional types (not typing.Optional)
def get_value(key: str) -> str | None:
    """Return value or None if not found."""
    ...

# Use union operator for unions (not typing.Union)
def parse_data(data: str | bytes) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Parse string or bytes data."""
    ...

# Use tuple for fixed-length sequences
def get_coordinates() -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Return x, y coordinates."""
    ...

docs.title: Type Hints Best Practices
docs.text:
• Always use type hints on public function parameters and return types
  • Use from __future__ import annotations at the top of modules
  • Prefer built-in types over typing module equivalents
  • Keep typing imports only for special types (Any, Callable, TypedDict, etc.)
  • Annotate complex types to improve code clarity
  • Use type aliases for complex or repeated types
from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Callable

# Type alias for clarity
PathLike = str | Path

def process_file(path: PathLike, callback: Callable[[str], Any]) -> None:
    """Process a file with a callback function."""
    ...

docs.title: Docstrings
docs.text: Format: PEP-257 compliant with Google-style parameter documentation

docs.title: Module Docstrings
docs.text:
"""Short module description.

Longer description providing more context about the module's purpose,
main functionality, and usage patterns.
"""

docs.title: Function Docstrings
docs.text:
def function_name(arg1: str, arg2: int) -> bool:
    """Short description of what the function does.

    Longer description if needed, explaining the function's behavior,
    side effects, or important implementation details.

    Args:
        arg1: Description of first argument
        arg2: Description of second argument

    Returns:
        Description of return value

    Raises:
        ValueError: When arg2 is negative
        FileNotFoundError: When file doesn't exist
    """
    ...

docs.title: Class Docstrings
docs.text:
class ClassName:
    """Short description of the class.

    Longer description explaining the class purpose, main responsibilities,
    and usage patterns.

    Attributes:
        attr1: Description of attribute
        attr2: Description of attribute
    """

    def __init__(self, param: str) -> None:
        """Initialize the class.

        Args:
            param: Description of parameter
        """
        ...

docs.title: Imports
docs.text:
Order:

  1. Standard library imports
  2. Third-party package imports
  3. Local application imports

Grouping: Separate each group with a blank line

Style: One import per line for clarity

# Standard library
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# Third-party
import click
import requests

# Local
from .core import process_file
from .data import load_data

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Type Convention Example Functions snake_case def process_data(): Variables snake_case user_name = "John" Constants UPPER_SNAKE_CASE MAX_SIZE = 100 Classes PascalCase class UserProfile: Exceptions PascalCase class ValidationError: Private _leading_underscore def _internal_func(): Protected _leading_underscore self._protected_attr Dunder __double_underscore__ __init__, __str__

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2025 Author Name <email@example.com>
# SPDX-License-Identifier: LICENSE-ID

"""Module docstring."""

from __future__ import annotations

# Imports (grouped as above)
import os
from pathlib import Path

# Constants
MAX_RETRIES = 3
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 30

# Type aliases
PathLike = str | Path

# Classes
class MyClass:
    ...

# Functions
def my_function():
    ...

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    ...

docs.title: Function Length
docs.text:
• Target: <20-30 lines per function
  • Rationale: Easier to understand, test, and maintain
  • Refactor long functions into smaller, focused helper functions

docs.title: Single Responsibility
docs.text:
• Each function should do one thing well
  • Each class should have one reason to change
  • Extract complex logic into separate functions

docs.title: String Formatting
docs.text:
Prefer f-strings:

# Good
name = "Alice"
message = f"Hello, {name}!"

# Avoid
message = "Hello, %s!" % name
message = "Hello, {}!".format(name)

docs.title: Path Handling
docs.text:
Prefer pathlib.Path:

from pathlib import Path

# Good
path = Path("data") / "file.txt"
if path.exists():
    content = path.read_text()

# Avoid
import os
path = os.path.join("data", "file.txt")
if os.path.exists(path):
    with open(path) as f:
        content = f.read()

docs.title: Context Managers
docs.text:
Always use with for resources:

# Good
with open("file.txt") as f:
    content = f.read()

# Avoid
f = open("file.txt")
content = f.read()
f.close()

docs.title: Comprehensions
docs.text:
Use when readable:

# Good - clear and concise
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
evens = [x for x in numbers if x % 2 == 0]

# Avoid - too complex
result = [
    process(x, y, z)
    for x in items
    for y in x.subitems
    for z in y.subsubitems
    if condition(x, y, z)
]

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Use for fixed sets:

from enum import Enum

class Status(Enum):
    PENDING = "pending"
    APPROVED = "approved"
    REJECTED = "rejected"

docs.title: EAFP vs LBYL
docs.text:
Prefer EAFP (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission):

# Good - EAFP
try:
    value = config[key]
except KeyError:
    value = default

# Avoid - LBYL
if key in config:
    value = config[key]
else:
    value = default

docs.title: Specific Exceptions
docs.text:
Catch specific exceptions:

# Good
try:
    data = json.loads(text)
except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
    logger.error(f"Invalid JSON: {exc}")
    raise

# Avoid
try:
    data = json.loads(text)
except Exception:
    pass

docs.title: Exception Context
docs.text:
Preserve exception context:

# Good
try:
    result = risky_operation()
except ValueError as exc:
    msg = f"Operation failed: {exc}"
    raise RuntimeError(msg) from exc

# Avoid
try:
    result = risky_operation()
except ValueError:
    raise RuntimeError("Operation failed")

docs.title: Formatter
docs.text:
black (line-length=99)

# Format all code
black src/ tests/

# Check formatting
black --check src/ tests/

docs.title: Linters
docs.text:
ruff (comprehensive linting)

# Lint code
ruff check src/ tests/

# Auto-fix issues
ruff check --fix src/ tests/

isort (import sorting)

# Sort imports
isort src/ tests/

# Check import order
isort --check-only src/ tests/

docs.title: Type Checker
docs.text:
mypy (static type checking)

# Type check code
mypy src/

# Strict mode
mypy --strict src/

docs.title: Quick Check
docs.text:
# Format, lint, and test
make quick-check

docs.title: Individual Checks
docs.text:
# Format code
make format

# Run linters
make lint

# Run type checker
mypy src/

# Run tests
make test

# Run all checks
make check

docs.title: pyproject.toml
docs.text:
[tool.black]
line-length = 99
target-version = ['py39']

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 99
target-version = "py39"
select = ["E", "F", "I", "N", "W"]

[tool.isort]
profile = "black"
line_length = 99

[tool.mypy]
python_version = "3.9"
strict = true
warn_return_any = true
warn_unused_configs = true

docs.title: Pre-commit Hooks
docs.text:
Install pre-commit hooks to automatically check code before committing:

# Install pre-commit
pip install pre-commit

# Install hooks
pre-commit install

# Run manually
pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: PEPs (Python Enhancement Proposals)
docs.text:
• PEP-8: Style Guide for Python Code
  • PEP-257: Docstring Conventions
  • PEP-484: Type Hints
  • PEP-585: Type Hinting Generics In Standard Collections
  • PEP-604: Allow writing union types as X | Y
  • PEP-563: Postponed Evaluation of Annotations

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Black: The uncompromising code formatter
  • Ruff: An extremely fast Python linter
  • mypy: Static type checker
  • isort: Import sorter

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This style guide ensures:

  • ✅ Consistent, readable code
  • ✅ Modern Python practices
  • ✅ Strong type safety
  • ✅ Maintainable codebase
  • ✅ Automated quality checks

Key Principles:

  1. Clarity over cleverness
  2. Explicit over implicit
  3. Simple over complex
  4. Tested over untested
  5. Documented over undocumented

docs.title: Contributing to RUNE
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing! RUNE is an early-stage but fast-moving project. Here’s how to get started.

docs.title: 🔧 Project Setup
docs.text:
1. Fork the repo and clone it locally:

bash git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/rune.git cd rune

  1. Set up a virtualenv:

bash python3 -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate pip install -e .[dev]

  1. Run CLI locally:

bash python rune_cli.py run noop --node testhost

docs.title: ✍️ Contributing a Plugin
docs.text:
1. Create a Bash script in plugins/
  2. Follow the Plugin Development Guide
  3. Test it with example JSON piped into stdin
  4. Make sure it returns structured JSON and exits correctly

docs.title: 🧪 Running Tests
docs.text:
pytest tests/

Add tests for any new modules you create under rune/ or plugins/.

docs.title: 🔁 Pull Request Process
docs.text:
• Fork → Feature branch → PR
  • Reference an issue (or create one)
  • Ensure all tests pass and no linting errors
  • Document any changes in docs/

docs.title: 🙏 Community Standards
docs.text: Be respectful. No drama. Fix bugs. Write useful things.

docs.title: 🔍 Where to Help
docs.text:
• Plugin development
  • CLI enhancements
  • SSM transport fallback
  • Plugin registry/metadata logic
  • Docs, tests, diagrams

Thanks for improving RUNE!

docs.title: PATENTS
docs.text:
As of this publication, no patents have been filed for any part of the RUNE project.

The RUNE framework, including its runtime architecture, orchestration design, plugin mediation patterns, and error-handling protocols, is publicly documented and published under an open license (MPL-2.0) as prior art.

This constitutes a defensive disclosure to prevent others from patenting the core framework, its communication protocols, or plugin interface contracts.

The project author retains all rights to pursue future defensive filings or claim original authorship if patent applications infringe upon this prior art.

If this project incorporates third-party packages that are themselves subject to patent grants, those are governed by their respective licenses (see pyproject.toml).

This document does not grant any patent licenses beyond those already implied or covered under MPL-2.0.

For any questions regarding licensing or intellectual property, contact the project maintainer directly.
